content: Through which prophet did the word of the LORD come in the book of Haggai?
explanation: Haggai 1:1 — 'In the second year of Darius the king, in the sixth month, on the first day of the month, the word of the LORD came by the hand of Haggai the prophet to Zerubbabel...'

content: According to Haggai 1:1, what office did Zerubbabel hold in Judah?
explanation: Haggai 1:1 — '...to Zerubbabel the son of Shealtiel, governor of Judah, and to Joshua the son of Jehozadak, the high priest...'

content: Who is Joshua in Haggai 1:1?
explanation: Haggai 1:1 — '...and to Joshua the son of Jehozadak, the high priest.' Zerubbabel and Joshua were the dual leaders of the returned community — civil and religious.
options: Son of Eliakim, teacher of the law
Son of Jehozadak, the high priest

content: According to Haggai 1:2, what excuse did the people give for delaying the rebuilding of the temple?
explanation: Haggai 1:2 — 'These people say the time has not yet come to rebuild the house of the LORD.'
options: The time has not yet come to rebuild the house of the LORD

content: According to Haggai 1:4, what does the LORD rebuke the people for?
explanation: Haggai 1:4 — 'Is it a time for you yourselves to dwell in your paneled houses, while this house lies in ruins?' They build their own houses while God's house lies in ruins — reversed priorities.
options: Dwelling in paneled houses while the temple lies in ruins

content: What command is repeated twice in Haggai 1:5 and 1:7?
explanation: Haggai 1:5, 7 — 'Now, therefore, thus says the LORD of hosts: Consider your ways.' Repeated twice — God calls His people to examine the consequences of misplaced priorities.

content: What image does Haggai 1:6 use to illustrate the futility of the people's labor?
explanation: Haggai 1:6 — '...he who earns wages does so to put them into a bag with holes.' Bag with holes — no matter how much they earn, it slips away; life lacks because God withholds blessing.
options: Pouring water into a broken jar
Wages put into a bag with holes
tags: Wages in a bag with holes

content: According to Haggai 1:8, where must the people go and what must they do?
explanation: Haggai 1:8 — 'Go up to the hills and bring wood and build the house, that I may take pleasure in it and that I may be glorified, says the LORD.'
options: Go to the sea to catch fish for the people
Go to foreign lands to buy grain
Go into the desert to seek treasure
Go up to the hills, bring wood, and build the LORD's house
tags: Go up, bring wood, build the house

content: After the people heeded the prophecy, what does Haggai 1:13 record the LORD saying?
explanation: Haggai 1:13 — 'Then Haggai, the messenger of the LORD, spoke to the people with the LORD's message, "I am with you, declares the LORD."' This is the core encouragement — God's presence.

content: What question does Haggai 2:3 pose to those who saw the former temple?
explanation: Haggai 2:3 — 'Who is left among you who saw this house in its former glory? How do you see it now? Is it not as nothing in your eyes?'
options: Do you remember the old miracles?
Who saw this house in its former glory?
Do you still have strength to work?
Who still remembers the former kings?

content: What exhortation does Haggai 2:4 repeat three times to the leaders and the people?
explanation: Haggai 2:4 — 'Yet now be strong, O Zerubbabel, declares the LORD. Be strong, O Joshua, son of Jehozadak, the high priest. Be strong, all you people of the land, declares the LORD. Work, for I am with you...'

content: According to Haggai 2:6, what will the LORD shake?
explanation: Haggai 2:6 — 'For thus says the LORD of hosts: Yet once more, in a little while, I will shake the heavens and the earth and the sea and the dry land.' Hebrews 12:26-27 quotes this to speak of eschatological renewal.
options: The heavens and the earth, the sea and the dry land

content: According to Haggai 2:7, with what will the LORD fill this house?
explanation: Haggai 2:7 — '...and the treasures of all nations shall come in, and I will fill this house with glory, says the LORD of hosts.'

content: According to Haggai 2:9, the latter glory of this house will be greater than what?
explanation: Haggai 2:9 — 'The latter glory of this house shall be greater than the former, says the LORD of hosts.' Fulfilled most fully when Christ — 'the desire of nations' — entered this temple.

content: According to Haggai 2:9, what will the LORD give in this place?
explanation: Haggai 2:9 — '...And in this place I will give peace, declares the LORD of hosts.'

content: According to Haggai 2:19, from the day the temple's rebuilding began, what does the LORD promise?
explanation: Haggai 2:19 — 'Is the seed yet in the barn? Indeed, the vine, the fig tree, the pomegranate, and the olive tree have yielded nothing. But from this day on I will bless you.' Obedience opens the door to blessing.
options: To give rain and wind as before
To bless them from this day on
tags: From this day I will bless

content: According to Haggai 2:23, what will the LORD make Zerubbabel like?
explanation: Haggai 2:23 — '...and I will make you like a signet ring, for I have chosen you, declares the LORD of hosts.' A signet ring was a sign of royal authority — Zerubbabel carried the messianic promise through David's line.

content: According to Haggai 1:6, which signs show the futility of the people's labor? (select all that apply)
explanation: Haggai 1:6 — 'You have sown much, and harvested little. You eat, but you never have enough; you drink, but you never have your fill... you clothe yourselves, but no one is warm... earns wages to put them into a bag with holes.' Multiple signs; 'building in one day' does not appear in this verse.
options: Eat but never satisfied, drink but never filled
Wages put into a bag with holes
Building a house in a single day

content: According to Haggai 2:4, to whom does the LORD say 'be strong'? (select all that apply)
explanation: Haggai 2:4 — 'Be strong, O Zerubbabel... Be strong, O Joshua, son of Jehozadak, the high priest. Be strong, all you people of the land...' Three recipients; Nehemiah is from the later era of Malachi and does not appear in Haggai.
options: All the people of the land

content: According to Haggai 1:10-11, what were the consequences of delaying the rebuilding of the temple? (select all that apply)
explanation: Haggai 1:10-11 — 'Therefore the heavens above you have withheld the dew, and the earth has withheld its produce. And I have called for a drought on the land and the hills, on the grain, the new wine, the oil...' Three clear consequences; 'locusts destroying vineyards' does not appear in this passage.
options: Drought on grain, new wine, and oil
